description: Korean target-language style guide rules for the document translation pipeline. Rules are designed to be composed by category instead of enumerating every purpose/formality/terminology combination.

priority_policy.description: Higher priority rules are rendered earlier in the prompt. Priorities express instruction importance, not execution order.

bands.meaning: Non-negotiable target-language quality and meaning-preservation rules.
Element-specific rules for table cells, labels, placeholders, roles, permissions, and task names.
Combination overrides that resolve conflicts between selected UI options.
Document-purpose rules that shape the translation for presentation, casual, business, or default use.

rules.rule: Preserve the source meaning, information density, and logical relationships. Do not add, omit, or reinterpret content.
Preserve the document structure and the function of each element. Translate headings as headings, list items as list items, and table cells compactly.
Use natural Korean spacing and orthography. Do not insert unnecessary spaces inside dates, quantities, units, or bound expressions.

Translate general role names, permission names, task names, table labels, field names, and UI-like labels into Korean unless they are proper nouns, acronyms, code identifiers, or explicitly preserved terminology.

For table cells, permission labels, role names, commands, and task labels, prefer short Korean noun phrases. Do not add sentence endings such as '-합니다' or '-습니다' unless the source is already a full sentence.
If the source item is the placeholder token 'BLANK', preserve 'BLANK' unchanged. Do not translate it as '공백' or expand it into explanatory text.

For abbreviated table labels or role names, use the table title, section heading, neighboring cells, and any ABBREVIATION_HINTS to resolve the abbreviation before translating. Do not choose an unrelated common expansion when local context indicates a specific meaning.
For role names, use a consistent Korean role-title pattern such as '관리자', '운영자', '감독자', '조회자', '승인자', '담당자', or '전문가' according to the source role.

For task labels beginning with verbs such as View, Read/View, Process, Manage, Configure, Create/Modify, Authorize, Download, or Approve, translate the action into a compact Korean label ending such as '조회', '읽기/조회', '처리', '관리', '구성', '생성/수정', '승인', or '다운로드'.

Do not leave ordinary English action words such as Process, Manage, View, Read/View, Configure, Create, Modify, Download, or Authorize in Korean task labels unless they are part of a proper noun or code identifier.
When no specific purpose is selected, translate into natural Korean while preserving the source style and document type.
For presentation materials, prefer concise, parallel wording that can be scanned quickly.
Do not expand slide titles, bullet points, chart labels, or axis labels into long full sentences unless the source already does so.
For everyday-use documents, use approachable Korean that general readers can understand naturally without sounding overly rigid.
For business documents, use concise and neutral Korean that preserves professionalism and credibility.
Use a formal Korean register. Maintain a polite and official tone in explanatory, report, administrative, and business contexts.
Use endings such as '-합니다', '-습니다', '-해야 합니다', or written-document endings such as '-이다' and '-한다' according to context.
Use natural and friendly Korean for informal style, but do not make the source information or expertise sound less serious.
Conversational Korean may be used for user guidance, explanations, or dialogue-like content, but avoid excessive slang or playful wording.

For source-language term preservation, keep proper nouns, brand names, product names, system names, company names, acronyms, code identifiers, API names, model names, and official named entities in the source language when translating them would be ambiguous or misleading.

Do not preserve ordinary English role names, permission names, task names, feature descriptions, table labels, or operational phrases merely because they look like business terms. Translate them into compact, natural Korean.
Translate the surrounding sentence naturally into Korean. Preserve a source term only when it is a named entity, acronym, identifier, or explicitly supplied terminology item.
For natural terminology translation, translate terms into natural Korean when doing so improves readability and does not lose business meaning.
Prefer common Korean expressions for general business, product, and operational terms instead of preserving English by default.
Still preserve proper nouns, brand names, acronyms, URLs, code identifiers, and terms that would become ambiguous or unnatural if translated.
For technical-term priority, prefer official, standard, or domain-established Korean terminology over casual paraphrases or ad-hoc natural wording.
Use provided terminology entries, glossary entries, termbase results, parallel-corpus conventions, or official documentation terms when available.
If no reliable Korean technical equivalent is available, preserve the source term or combine it with a concise Korean descriptor rather than inventing a new term.

overrides.rule: For informal presentation materials, keep slide text short and phrase-based; do not over-convert it into casual conversational sentences.
When business purpose is selected, even informal style must avoid overly personal wording and remain suitable for workplace documents.
